left-right-join.label: LEFT and RIGHT JOIN
left-right-join.summary: Learn how to use LEFT and RIGHT JOIN to include unmatched rows from one table when combining data from multiple tables.

sketch0.title: What are LEFT and RIGHT JOINs?

sketch2.title: When to Use LEFT or RIGHT JOIN

sk1.title: What are LEFT and RIGHT JOINs?
sk1.bodyMarkdown:
When combining tables in SQL, sometimes you want to keep all rows from one table, even if there is no matching row in the other table. This is where **LEFT JOIN** and **RIGHT JOIN** come in.

- **LEFT JOIN** returns all rows from the left table, and matching rows from the right table. If there is no match, columns from the right table will be NULL.
- **RIGHT JOIN** is the opposite: it returns all rows from the right table, and matching rows from the left table. If there is no match, columns from the left table will be NULL.

**Example:**

Suppose you want to list all courses and any enrollments for 'Spring 2026'. Some courses may not have any enrollments yet.

```sql
SELECT courses.title, enrollments.student_id
FROM courses
LEFT JOIN enrollments ON courses.id = enrollments.course_id
  AND enrollments.term = 'Spring 2026';
```

This query lists every course, showing the student_id if someone is enrolled, or NULL if not.

sk2.title: Visualizing LEFT JOIN
sk2.bodyMarkdown:
Imagine two tables:

- **courses**: All possible courses
- **enrollments**: Records of students enrolled in courses

A **LEFT JOIN** from courses to enrollments ensures every course appears in the result, even if no one is enrolled. If a course has no enrollments, the enrollment columns will be NULL for that row.

This is useful for finding items that might not have related records, like courses with no students.

sk3.title: When to Use LEFT or RIGHT JOIN
sk3.bodyMarkdown:
- Use **LEFT JOIN** when you want to keep all rows from the first (left) table.
- Use **RIGHT JOIN** when you want to keep all rows from the second (right) table.

In SQLite, RIGHT JOIN is not directly supported, but you can swap the table order and use LEFT JOIN to achieve the same result.

q1.title: Understanding LEFT JOIN
q1.prompt: What does a LEFT JOIN do when there are rows in the left table with no matching rows in the right table?
q1.hint: Think about what happens to unmatched rows from the left table.

help.concept: LEFT JOIN keeps all rows from the left table, filling in NULLs for missing matches from the right table.
help.hint_1: Unmatched left table rows are not dropped.
help.hint_2: If there is no match in the right table, the right table's columns are NULL.

options.a: It excludes those rows from the result.
options.b: It includes those rows, with NULLs for columns from the right table.
options.d: It only includes rows that match in both tables.

q2.title: Choosing the Correct JOIN
q2.prompt: In which scenarios would you use a LEFT JOIN? (Select all that apply.)
q2.hint: LEFT JOIN is useful when you want to keep all rows from one table, even if there are no matches in the other.

help.concept: LEFT JOIN is used to include all rows from the left table, regardless of whether there is the correct answer matching row in the right table.
help.hint_1: Think about whether you want to keep unmatched rows from the left table.
help.hint_2: LEFT JOIN is for cases where you want to see all entries from the left table, even if they have no related records.

options.a: To find all students and any courses they are enrolled in, even if some students are not enrolled in any course.
options.b: To find only students who are enrolled in at least one course.
options.c: To list all courses, showing which students are enrolled, including courses with no enrollments.
options.d: To list only courses that have enrollments.

q3.title: LEFT JOIN Query Structure
q3.prompt: Arrange the SQL keywords to form a correct LEFT JOIN query that lists all courses and their department names.
q3.hint: Start with SELECT, then FROM, then the JOIN clause.

help.concept: A LEFT JOIN query starts with SELECT, then FROM, then the JOIN and ON clauses.
help.hint_1: SELECT comes first, then FROM, then JOIN.
help.hint_2: The ON clause specifies how the tables are related.

q4.title: Fill in the JOIN Type
q4.hint: You want to keep all students, even those without enrollments.

help.concept: the missing term keeps all rows from the left table, even if there are no matches in the right table.
help.hint_1: Which JOIN type keeps all rows from the first table?
help.hint_2: the missing term is used to include all students, even if they have no enrollments.

q4.template:
SELECT students.name, enrollments.course_id
FROM students [___] enrollments ON students.id = enrollments.student_id;

q5.title: Find Courses Without Enrollments
q5.prompt: Write a SQL query to list the titles of all courses that have no enrollments for 'Spring 2026'.
q5.hint: Use LEFT JOIN and filter for rows where there is no matching enrollment.

help.concept: LEFT JOIN can be used to find rows in one table that do not have related rows in another table by checking for NULLs.
help.hint_1: LEFT JOIN courses to enrollments, then look for enrollments that are missing.
help.hint_2: Check for NULL in the enrollments table to find courses with no enrollments.

q5.starterCode:
-- List course titles with no enrollments in Spring 2026
SELECT
